planting.note: Divide established clumps every 4–5 years in spring or plant container-grown plants in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; Agapanthus inapertus is more cold-hardy than the common Agapanthus praecox, surviving to zone 7 with mulch protection. It blooms later (August–September) than other species, extending the agapanthus season. The pendant, tubular flowers are distinctively different from the more open, funnel-shaped flowers of A. praecox.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; drooping agapanthus is a South African species that extends the agapanthus season into August and September, blooming a full month or more after most agapanthus species have finished. The flowers are distinctively pendant (drooping) and tubular — nodding downward from the umbel rather than opening broadly upward as in Agapanthus praecox — and are an intensely deep, midnight-blue to violet-blue, one of the richest blues achievable in any summer perennial. This combination of late bloom, pendant flower form, and exceptional depth of color makes it unlike any other agapanthus. The narrow, strap-like leaves are somewhat more deciduous than evergreen species. It is one of the more cold-hardy agapanthus species, tolerating zone 7 with adequate mulch. Excellent as a late-season focal point in mixed borders and for extending the agapanthus display well into late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in fall in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; flame freesia grows through the cool season and blooms in spring in a Mediterranean cycle. In zone 8–10 it naturalizes as a perennial; in zone 7 and colder, lift corms after foliage dies and store dry through summer. It performs similarly to freesia in culture but is more heat-tolerant.

directions: Plant corms in fall in very well-drained, sandy soil in full sun; flame freesia is a South African corm related to ixia and freesia, producing upright stems of 8–12 brilliantly-colored, cup-shaped flowers in vivid shades of orange, red, or pink-salmon — the intense warm-orange forms being particularly striking and giving rise to the common name 'flame freesia.' The fan-like arrangement of narrow, sword-shaped leaves is typical of the iris family and attractive before bloom. Growing on a Mediterranean cycle (fall planting, cool-season growth, spring bloom, summer dormancy), flame freesia naturalizes easily in well-drained Mediterranean and Gulf Coast gardens where the soil dries in summer. The brilliant warm colors make it outstanding for combining with blue or purple spring-blooming companions. It is more tolerant of heat and humidity than many South African bulbs, adapting well to the Gulf Coast climate where it grows and blooms from late spring.

planting.note: Plant large bulbs in fall with the nose just at or slightly below the soil surface in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Portuguese squill naturalizes readily in mild climates and spreads slowly by offsets. In zone 8–10 it is reliably perennial and requires no lifting. The misleading name 'Cuban Lily' and 'peruviana' (Peruvian) both result from historical mis-labeling — this plant is actually native to the western Mediterranean.

directions: Plant large bulbs in fall in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Portuguese squill is a large Mediterranean bulb producing one of the most impressive spring floral displays of any small bulb — a massive, domed to conical cluster of 50–100 or more small, star-shaped, vivid blue-purple flowers densely packed into a broad, pyramidal head on a thick, fleshy stem. The overall effect is of a rich, blue-purple dome of stars, 4–6 inches across, carried above a spreading rosette of wide, strap-like leaves. Despite the species name 'peruviana,' it is native to the western Mediterranean (Spain, Portugal, and North Africa) — the name resulted from a 17th-century botanical error. Naturalizes readily in mild climates (zones 8–11) in well-drained soil and slowly multiplies by offsets to form impressive clumps. Unlike the tiny spring squills (Scilla siberica), this is a large, bold bulb that makes a significant visual statement. Goes completely dormant by summer in true Mediterranean bulb fashion.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, neutral soil in full sun; orange star grows on a Mediterranean cycle — cool-season growth, spring bloom, summer dormancy — and naturalizes in zone 9–11. In zone 7–8, lift bulbs after foliage yellows and store dry through summer. It is also widely grown as a cut flower and an indoor pot plant.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, neutral soil in full sun; orange star is a South African Ornithogalum producing clusters of brilliant orange to yellow-orange, star-shaped flowers — entirely atypical for the genus, which is almost exclusively white-flowered — that are long-lasting both in the garden and as cut flowers. The dense, dome-shaped clusters of orange stars are vivid and eye-catching in late winter to spring. As a South African Cape bulb, it grows through the cool season, blooms in late winter to spring, and goes dormant in summer — requiring dry conditions during its summer dormancy to prevent bulb rot. In zone 9–11 it naturalizes as a perennial in well-drained, sunny beds. It is one of the most widely sold cut flower bulbs globally. Despite its common name 'Orange Star,' the flower color varies from bright orange to yellow-orange to orange-red depending on the clone, with the most vivid orange forms being the most ornamental. Long-lasting in a vase — up to 3 weeks — making it an outstanding cut flower.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings easily by allowing cut ends to callus for a few days, then placing in moist orchid bark or perlite; mistletoe cactus is the only cactus native to the Old World (occurring in tropical Africa and Sri Lanka as well as South America) and requires bright indirect light — NOT direct sun, which bleaches the stems. It grows outdoors in zone 10–11 in shade and as a houseplant in bright indirect light in all zones.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light in orchid bark or very well-drained, humus-rich medium; mistletoe cactus is the world's most widely distributed cactus species — uniquely occurring across tropical South America, Central America, the Caribbean, tropical Africa, and even Sri Lanka, making it the only cactus native to the Old World. It is an epiphyte of tropical forest canopies, hanging in long, pendulous, leafless green stems that resemble pencil-thin ropes cascading from tree branches, much like mistletoe (hence the common name). In late winter to spring, tiny, inconspicuous cream-white flowers appear along the stem margins, followed by translucent white berries resembling mistletoe berries. Unlike most cacti, it requires indirect light, moderate humidity, and more frequent watering — much more like an orchid than a desert plant. Excellent as a dramatic hanging basket plant in bright, indirect indoor light or outdoors in frost-free shade. It tolerates neglect and low-light conditions that kill most plants.
